---
title: "Washington Highway CMF Analysis"
subtitle: "Hierarchical Crash Modification Factors"
author: "MetaCount Research Group"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
    fig-align: center
    highlight-style: github
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.08
    css: styles-cmf.css
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Teal / Orange palette overrides ── */
:root {
  --teal:   #0a6c74;
  --orange: #d96f32;
  --ink:    #152238;
  --muted:  #5d6b79;
  --paper:  #f8f6f2;
  --green:  #2a7f4f;
  --red:    #b93a2b;
}

/* Slide headings */
.reveal h1, .reveal h2 { color: var(--teal); }
.reveal h3             { color: var(--orange); }

/* Accent bar under h2 */
.reveal h2::after {
  content: "";
  display: block;
  height: 3px;
  width: 60px;
  background: var(--orange);
  margin-top: 6px;
  border-radius: 2px;
}

/* Hero title slide */
.reveal .title-slide h1 { font-size: 2.1rem; color: #fff; }
.reveal .title-slide    { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #0a6c74 0%, #18435a 55%, #8a4e2c 100%); }

/* Stat box */
.stat-box {
  background: var(--paper);
  border-left: 4px solid var(--teal);
  border-radius: 6px;
  padding: 10px 16px;
  margin: 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.92rem;
}
.stat-box .num { font-size: 2rem; font-weight: 800; color: var(--teal); }
.stat-box .lbl { color: var(--muted); font-size: 0.78rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em; }

/* Variable table */
table { font-size: 0.82rem; }
th    { background: var(--teal); color: #fff; padding: 5px 10px; }
td    { padding: 4px 10px; }
tr:nth-child(even) td { background: #eef6f7; }

/* CMF formula block */
.formula-box {
  background: #eef6f7;
  border: 1px solid #b5d9db;
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 14px 20px;
  font-size: 1rem;
  margin: 10px 0;
}

/* Callout-style highlight */
.callout {
  border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);
  background: #fff8f3;
  padding: 10px 16px;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.88rem;
  margin: 8px 0;
}

/* iframe full-width */
iframe {
  border: none;
  border-radius: 8px;
  box-shadow: 0 4px 14px rgba(0,0,0,0.12);
}

/* Bullet refinement */
.reveal ul li { margin-bottom: 0.4em; }
</style>
```

## Data Overview {#data-overview}

### Washington Rural Highway Safety Dataset

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">275</div><div class="lbl">road segments</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">39</div><div class="lbl">variables per segment</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">FREQ</div><div class="lbl">crash count response</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">AADT</div><div class="lbl">primary traffic exposure</div></div>

Source: Washington State DOT rural highway inventory, Ex-16-3.csv
:::

::: {.column width="52%"}
| Category | Variables |
|---|---|
| **Traffic** | AADT, PEAKHR, ADTLANE, EXPOSE |
| **Geometry** | INCLANES, DECLANES, WIDTH, MEDWIDTH |
| **Alignment** | CURVES, MINRAD, TANGENT, GRADEBR |
| **Grade** | MIGRADE, MXGRADE, MXGRDIFF, SLOPE |
| **Roadside** | MIMEDSH, MXMEDSH, ACCESS, FRICTION |
| **Climate** | AVEPRE, AVESNOW, HISNOW, LOWPRE |
| **Classification** | URB, FC, SINGLE, DOUBLE, TRAIN |
| **Interaction** | INTPM, CPM, INTECHAG |
:::

::::

---

## Data Quality {#data-quality}

### Key Variable Summaries

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Crash frequency (FREQ)**

- Range: 0 – high overdispersion typical of count data
- Mean ≈ 1–3 crashes per segment-year (dataset-dependent)
- Zero-inflation is modest; NB/Poisson both viable
- LENGTH (segment length) used as exposure offset via log(LENGTH)

**AADT distribution**

- Spans rural to semi-urban traffic volumes
- Log transformation used in model specification
- Monotone AADT elasticity enforced during search
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Data preparation steps**

1. Read `Ex-16-3.csv` (275 rows, 39 cols)
2. Compute `OFFSET = log(LENGTH)` for exposure
3. Drop rows with missing FREQ or AADT
4. Require AADT > 0
5. Assign OBS_ID sequential index

**Candidate variables after filtering**

- 31 upper-level candidates (direct effects)
- 9 lower-level candidates (AADT interaction terms)
- Binary vars get toggle treatment (no standardization)
- Continuous vars standardized on train set (zero mean, unit SD)
:::

::::

---

## Methodology {#methodology}

### Hierarchical CMF Count Model

The model specifies a **two-level hierarchical** log-linear count model:

$$
\log(\hat{\mu}_i) = \alpha + \underbrace{\beta_{\text{AADT}} \log(\text{AADT}_i)}_{\text{base AADT effect}}
  + \underbrace{\sum_{j \in U} \beta_j x_{ij}}_{\text{upper-level terms}}
  + \underbrace{\sum_{k \in L} \gamma_k x_{ik} \log(\text{AADT}_i)}_{\text{lower-level (AADT interaction) terms}}
  + \text{offset}_i
$$

where $\hat{\mu}_i$ is the expected crash count for segment $i$.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Upper-level terms** $U$

Additive effects on log-crash-rate, independent of traffic volume.
Examples: road geometry, lane counts, pavement friction, climate.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Lower-level terms** $L$

Modify the **AADT elasticity** — how crash risk scales with volume.
Examples: curvature, friction, grade, precipitation.

$$\text{Local AADT elasticity}_i = \beta_{\text{AADT}} + \sum_{k \in L} \gamma_k x_{ik}$$
:::

::::

<div class="callout">
CMF for variable $j$ at a given AADT: $\text{CMF}(x_j) = \exp(\beta_j \cdot (x_j - x_j^{\text{ref}}))$ for upper terms, with AADT-varying response for lower terms.
</div>

---

## Search Strategy {#search-strategy}

### Random Search over Variable Subsets

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
**Algorithm**

1. Seed with two baseline models (single-var and null)
2. For each of **120 iterations**:
   - Draw $k_U \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 6]$ upper vars
   - Draw $k_L \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 5]$ lower vars
   - Sample without replacement from candidate pools
   - Fit Poisson/NB model on **training split** (60%)
   - Score on **validation split** (20%) by Poisson deviance
   - Track AADT elasticity — enforce monotonicity constraint
3. Select best monotonic model; fall back if none found
4. **Final refit** on train + validation (80%)
5. Report metrics on **held-out test split** (20%)
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Scoring metrics**

| Metric | Use |
|---|---|
| Val Poisson Deviance | Primary selection criterion |
| BIC | Complexity-penalized fit |
| Val RMSE | Secondary diagnostic |
| AADT elasticity min | Monotonicity guard |

**Why Poisson deviance?**

Proper scoring rule for count data. Penalizes both over- and under-prediction relative to mean, and is consistent for mean estimation even under overdispersion.

**Why enforce monotonicity?**

Road safety theory requires more traffic → more crashes (positive elasticity). Models violating this are physically implausible.
:::

::::

---

## Convergence {#convergence}

### Search Convergence — BIC and Validation Deviance

<iframe
  src="../results/ex16_3_cmf/search_convergence.html"
  width="100%"
  height="500px"
  title="Search convergence plot">
  <p><em>search_convergence.html not yet rendered — run the Python script first.</em></p>
</iframe>

::: {.callout}
**Green dots** = monotonic AADT models (eligible for selection).
**Grey dots** = non-monotonic (excluded unless fallback enabled).
**Star** = best-ever model. Thick line = running minimum.
:::

---

## Selected Model {#selected-model}

### Best Model Specification

After search and final refit on 80% of data:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Model form**

- Family: Poisson (or NB2 if `--family nb`)
- Offset: log(LENGTH)
- Upper-level variables: selected subset from 31 candidates
- Lower-level variables: selected subset from 9 candidates

**Coefficient table** (see `coefficients_standardized_and_original.csv`)

| Parameter | Component | Scale |
|---|---|---|
| const | core | native |
| log_aadt | core | native |
| SPEED_Z | upper | std. continuous |
| CURVES_Z | upper | std. continuous |
| CURVES_Z × log(AADT) | lower | std. interaction |
| … | … | … |

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Key model properties**

<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">✓</div><div class="lbl">Positive AADT elasticity everywhere in dataset</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">BIC</div><div class="lbl">Model complexity penalty satisfied</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">80%</div><div class="lbl">Data used in final coefficient estimation</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">20%</div><div class="lbl">Held out for unbiased test evaluation</div></div>

**Output files**

- `final_model_spec.json` — full coefficient dictionary
- `coefficients_standardized_and_original.md` — human-readable table
- `final_model_fit.pkl` — serialised statsmodels result
:::

::::

---

## CMF Interpretation {#cmf-interpretation}

### What Each CMF Represents

A **Crash Modification Factor (CMF)** answers: *if this road characteristic changes, by how much does crash risk multiply?*

$$\text{CMF} = \frac{\hat{\mu}(\text{modified})}{\hat{\mu}(\text{baseline})}$$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Upper-level CMF (AADT-independent)**

Scaling factor is constant across traffic levels.

Example — CURVES:
- More curves → higher CMF (CMF > 1 → more crashes)
- Effect size fixed regardless of AADT

**Lower-level CMF (AADT-varying)**

Scaling factor changes with traffic volume.

Example — FRICTION × log(AADT):
- At low AADT: friction effect modest
- At high AADT: friction deficiency multiplied

This captures how the same road deficiency is more dangerous on a busy road.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Illustrative example: CURVES at three AADT levels**

| CURVES (z-score) | Low AADT | Median AADT | High AADT |
|---|---|---|---|
| −1 SD (few curves) | 0.78 | 0.82 | 0.87 |
| 0 (median) | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| +1 SD (many curves) | 1.28 | 1.22 | 1.15 |

*Values shown are illustrative; actual values depend on fitted coefficients.*

<div class="callout">
The AADT-varying CMF is the core innovation of the hierarchical model: it captures **effect modification** between geometry and traffic volume.
</div>
:::

::::

---

## AADT Response {#aadt-response}

### Observed vs. Predicted by AADT

<iframe
  src="../results/ex16_3_cmf/aadt_obs_pred.html"
  width="100%"
  height="500px"
  title="AADT observed vs predicted">
  <p><em>aadt_obs_pred.html not yet rendered — run the Python script first.</em></p>
</iframe>

Top panel shows raw observed crashes (coloured by train/val/test split) and the binned-smooth predicted line. Bottom panel shows normalised residuals $(pred - obs) / \max(obs, 1)$ — orange = over-predicted, teal = under-predicted. Background shading marks the p10–p25 and p75–p90 AADT quantile bands.

---

## Interactive Dashboard {#interactive-dashboard}

### CMF Explorer — Adjust Inputs, See Live Predictions

<iframe
  src="../results/ex16_3_cmf/hierarchical_cmf_dashboard.html"
  width="100%"
  height="500px"
  title="Hierarchical CMF Dashboard">
  <p><em>hierarchical_cmf_dashboard.html not yet rendered — run the Python script first.</em></p>
</iframe>

Use the **variable dropdown** to select which CMF curve to display, toggle between *Predicted crashes* and *CMF* modes, and adjust the AADT slider. Each variable control shows a live **CMF badge** (e.g. CMF: 0.84 −16%). The **Prediction Summary** panel shows the current total predicted crash count and AADT elasticity.

---

## Random Parameters {#random-parameters}

### Capturing Unobserved Heterogeneity

Standard fixed-effects models assume all sites respond identically to the same covariate value. In practice, **unobserved site characteristics** cause the same geometry to have different effects across locations.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
**Random-parameters extension**

$$\beta_{ij} = \bar{\beta}_j + \eta_{ij}, \quad \eta_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_j^2)$$

- Each segment $i$ gets its own draw of $\beta_j$
- Estimated via simulated maximum likelihood or Bayesian HMC
- Uses `ExperimentBuilder.fit_manual_model` if available

**Implementation in this script**

- Fixed terms: log(AADT), upper vars (standardised), lower interaction terms
- Random (independent) terms: continuous upper vars
- Correlated random terms: top-2 continuous lower vars (captures co-movement between e.g. curvature and friction effects)
- Dispersion = 1 (NB2 parameterisation)
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Benefits**

<div class="stat-box"><div class="lbl">Correct standard errors</div>Fixed-parameter SEs are too small when heterogeneity is ignored.</div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="lbl">Segment-specific elasticities</div>Each site has its own AADT elasticity posterior.</div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="lbl">Better out-of-sample calibration</div>Shrinkage regularises extreme coefficient estimates.</div>

**Output**

- `random_params_summary.json` written if ExperimentBuilder is installed
- Contains full summary text of the mixed model

*Note: requires optional `experiment_package` dependency. The script falls back gracefully if unavailable.*
:::

::::

---

## Key Findings {#key-findings}

### Why Hierarchical CMFs Matter

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
**Methodological contributions**

- Unified framework for estimating both **level** and **AADT-sensitivity** of crash risk
- Random search over variable subsets avoids specification bias from manual stepwise selection
- Monotonicity constraint on AADT elasticity enforces physical plausibility
- Train / validation / test split prevents over-fitting and gives honest performance estimates
- Standardised coefficients enable direct comparison of effect magnitudes across variables

**Practical benefits for safety analysts**

- Interactive CMF dashboard lets practitioners explore *what-if* scenarios without re-running models
- AADT-varying CMFs distinguish high-volume from low-volume road risk profiles
- Binary variable toggle buttons clearly communicate on/off safety treatments
- Convergence plot diagnoses search quality and identifies if more iterations are needed
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Selected variable categories**

Candidate pool expanded to include:

| Group | Variables |
|---|---|
| Lanes | INCLANES, DECLANES |
| Geometry | CURVES, MINRAD, TANGENT |
| Grade | MIGRADE, MXGRADE, MXGRDIFF |
| Median | MIMEDSH, MXMEDSH, MEDWIDTH |
| Surface | FRICTION, SLOPE |
| Climate | AVEPRE, AVESNOW, HISNOW, LOWPRE |
| Traffic | PEAKHR, ADTLANE, EXPOSE, INTPM, CPM |
| Class | URB, FC, SINGLE, DOUBLE, TRAIN |

All filtered to columns present in dataset before search.
:::

::::

---

## Conclusions {#conclusions}

### Why This Matters for Safety Planning

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
**The hierarchical CMF framework provides:**

1. **Transparent model structure** — the two-level log-linear form is interpretable by practitioners and directly produces CMFs
2. **AADT-varying effects** — safety treatments that have different effectiveness under light vs. heavy traffic can be correctly estimated and communicated
3. **Automated variable selection** with explainable search history and convergence diagnostics
4. **Reproducible workflow** — single Python script, CSV input, all outputs written to a dated results directory
5. **Interactive communication tool** — the HTML dashboard makes model outputs accessible to non-statistician decision-makers

**Limitations and future work**

- Random search does not guarantee global optimum; Bayesian optimisation or full enumeration could improve selection
- Correlated random parameters require `ExperimentBuilder` (optional dependency)
- Temporal dynamics (year effects, trend) not currently modelled
- Spatial autocorrelation between nearby segments not explicitly addressed
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Output file summary**

| File | Content |
|---|---|
| `hierarchical_cmf_dashboard.html` | Interactive CMF explorer |
| `search_convergence.html` | BIC/deviance convergence |
| `aadt_obs_pred.html` | Obs vs pred by AADT |
| `final_model_spec.json` | Coefficients + metadata |
| `validation_metrics.csv` | Train/val/test metrics |
| `search_history_full.csv` | All 120+ candidates |
| `coefficients_*.csv` | Parameter estimates |
| `random_params_summary.json` | Mixed model (optional) |

**Run the experiment**

```bash
python scripts/generate_washington_hierarchical_cmf_assets.py \
  --input data/Ex-16-3.csv \
  --output-dir results/ex16_3_cmf \
  --search-iter 120 \
  --family poisson \
  --allow-nonmonotonic-fallback
```
:::

::::
